body:
Hi, so this statement is still accurate in that we don't send the `redirects` to the client to use them during resolving client-side navigations. The case where `redirects` can fail with `next/link` is when you have a `redirect` that should take priority over a client-side route like a `rewrite` or a page e.g. if we have a redirect `{ source: '/first', destination: '/somewhere' }` and then a page `pages/[slug].js` then `next/link` will navigate to `[slug]` since it matches. 

If a page or `rewrite` is not matched client-side then Next.js will do a hard navigation which will then trigger the redirect to apply server-side. 

Maybe we can tweak this note to make the above scenario where they won't take affect more clear

body:
Each of our Next applications have a `babelrc` that looks like this:

```
plugins: [
  [
    'relay',
    {
      src: './',
      artifactDirectory: '../../artifactsDirectory',
      extensions: ['js', 'ts', 'tsx'],
      include: ['**/*'],
      schema: '../apps/backend/schemas/__generated__/main.graphql',
      watchman: false,
      language: 'typescript',
    },
  ],
],
```

We then have a custom watcher that runs @ the root and watches code in the different applications and manually re-invoke `relay-compiler`.

---

It might _just work_ as long as what is recorded in every projects `artifactsDirectory` is written to the source files, in the past I've tested SWC with a pretty naive approach, running jscodeshift and changing my codebase (removing the need for babel): https://gist.github.com/hanford/003f07533f7bfc8af7fc90a838f0e963#file-jscodeshift-relay-js-L23

Happy to set up a repro if it's a usecase that doesn't _just work_ though.

body:
@tbezman Sure! A developer changed the fragment or a query (added/changed field, etc), but didn't run the `relay-compiler`. When they refresh the page, the generated artifact for this file (.graphql.js file) won't contain the changes, this sometimes may lead to confusing situations, when developers may not see changes they are expecting on the screen.

To reduce the confusion, In the babel plugin we're comping the `md5` hash of the text inside `graphql` tag. And we're comparing this hash to the `hash` in the generated artifact (created by the compiler), if these hashes are different - we're showing a warning in the browser's console. When developers debug the outdated views, they may see the reason in the console.

body:
```suggestion
You should use `getServerSideProps` only if you need to pre-render a page whose data must be fetched at request time. [Time to First Byte (TTFB)](/learn/seo/web-performance) will be higher than [`getStaticProps`](/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-static-props.md) because the server must compute the result on every request, and the result can only be cached by a CDN using `cache-control` headers (which could require extra configuration).
```

This is correct as-is, just not when you view it in GitHub, which is generally not used.
author: timneutkens

body: Good catch, thanks!
author: balazsorban44

body:
```suggestion
When combined with [Incremental Static Regeneration](/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/incremental-static-regeneration.md), `getStaticProps` will run in the background while the stale page is being revalidated, and the fresh page served to the browser.

Because `getStaticProps` runs at build time, it does **not** have access to the incoming request (such as query parameters or `HTTP` headers) as it generates static `HTML`. If you need access to the request for your page, consider using [Middleware](/docs/middleware.md) in addition to `getStaticProps`.
```

body: The weird thing here is that the 'lazyRoot' prop as a RefObject, has its "current" attribute populated only after the parent mounts and the (good or bad) thing is that when it  DOES change from 'null' and points to the element, it does not trigger re-rendering. That means that either we need to render our image only after the parent element mounts (with a usage of "useEffect" on the parent by the developer), or after the Image element mounts. So in my opinion it would be a burden for the developer to do all that so I went with the second approach. Removing the useEffect will cause the "should load the image when the lazyRoot prop is used" test to fail
author: 11koukou

body:
Thanks a lot for your help!
But I may not understand what you mean?

<hr />

During SSR, There will only render `<AppContainer> from server/render.tsx`, 

but NOT render for `<AppContainer> from client/index.tsx`.

So, if I put `ImageConfigContext` to `<AppContainer> from client/index.tsx`, the error will still be thrown during SSR:

![7235abaf52187d87f1181bd77c55129](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/14243906/152572723-93ad85a7-19d8-428f-bc35-81ead3445dc7.png)


Can I:
1. Skip `defaultLoader` config check during SSR? eg: `if (typeof window !== undefined)`.
2. Put `<RuntimeImageConfigContext.Provider>` to `<AppContainer> from server/render.tsx`.

What's your suggestion? Am I missing something?
author: JuniorTour

body:
@JuniorTour Just to be clear, `ImageConfigContext` needs to included in **both** files:

- `next/server/render.tsx`
- `next/client/index.tsx`

Right around these lines:

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0b1d5e17bc304ba9bbc2cafc9b370bc66e359e7d/packages/next/server/render.tsx#L715

I found the `renderOpts.images` is `string` during SSR, so I have to use `JSON.parse()`,

Am I miss something?

![ebab93c4bf75c488f57522f3c163380](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/14243906/152645869-16f3f052-cbd5-4ef3-bb89-a40664079c6d.png)

body: Thats very odd. The other properties on renderOpts support non-string types so there must be something missing. What about assigning that property `images: process.env.__NEXT_IMAGE_OPTS` everywhere the `const renderOpts = { ...` is used? That way webpack can replace the env var with the object.
author: styfle

body: Also, what happens when you try `value={process.env.__NEXT_IMAGE_OPTS as any as ImageConfigComplete}` here like you did the for client?
author: styfle

body:
Even if we when use `value={process.env.__NEXT_IMAGE_OPTS}`, the value will still be undefined.

Maybe because the server side code is not compiled by Webpack?

![1f289dd25754400fd4e3c29acc1b118](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/14243906/152739805-7568d536-212b-46b0-bd55-6909a0aeddb8.png)

<hr/>


But I found `renderOpts.image` was set to string in `base-server.ts` :
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/938eb0e28c2615f64c1d26ea400b7a2cfb562dbe/packages/next/server/base-server.ts#L298

[When substitute`images: JSON.stringify(this.nextConfig.images),` with `images: this.nextConfig.images,` , there is no error, it passed all test case](https://github.com/JuniorTour/next.js/pull/4/files#diff-2b8e3503c1bebf856610a5551d9c5996435b99f45db389d4eeac1b671518875eL298)

<hr/>

So Can I remove the `JSON.stringify()` for `images: JSON.stringify(this.nextConfig.images),` in `base-server.ts` ?

So that we can use `<RuntimeImageConfigContext.Provider value={images} />` without JSON.parse().

body:
```suggestion
When a request is made to a path that hasn’t been generated, Next.js will server-render the page on the first request. Future requests will serve the static file from the cache. ISR on Vercel [persists the cache globally and handles rollbacks](https://vercel.com/docs/concepts/next.js/incremental-static-regeneration).
```

I think we're looking into ways of running `relay-compiler` in the browser. One live example is this https://relay.dev/compiler-explorer/ - it runs a special version of compiler, compiled to wasm, and we could theoretically use this as a foundation for the full-featured relay compiler in `wasm`. But for now, yes - I'll send a PR to restore these `cfg` changes.

```suggestion
You should use `getServerSideProps` only if you need to pre-render a page whose data must be fetched at request time. [Time to First Byte (TTFB)](https://nextjs.org/learn/seo/web-performance) will be higher than [`getStaticProps`](/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-static-props.md) because the server must compute the result on every request, and the result can only be cached by a CDN using `cache-control` headers (which could require extra configuration).
```

body:
We might need to tweak how `makePublicRouterInstance` is handling the fields to prevent this from being re-created on every render. Currently it's creating a new instance and copying the fields to prevent them from being treated as stateful. 

This also causes the router methods to change on each render, related issue here https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/18127

body: Doesn't putting it outside of render and creating it once at initialization, like this PR does, mitigate the issue?

body: Oh, looks like we should go with https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/33875 approach instead? @devknoll
author: gaearon

body: It does but then the fields are never updated from the initial public router since they are copied here https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/39d3210776d37e7dc11e77b54a65b33b5454c0ce/packages/next/client/router.ts#L160-L170 and then the underlying router instance is updated but not the public router instance. So maybe instead of copying the fields we could Proxy them in development, erroring when setting to them to prevent them from being treated as stateful and in production we don't modify the router like this 🤔
author: ijjk

body: So, the issue is that _router_ is a bit of a misnomer here. When used globally, it means "routing functions + a reference to the currently visible route." When used locally (via a React hook), it means "routing functions + the currently rendered route." The router context _should_ change for each tree IMO. Consider something like this: ```jsx function Foo() { const { query } = useRouter() return {JSON.stringify(query)} } ``` 1. If the context didn't change when `query` changed, how would `Foo` rerender? 2. If the value of `query` only pointed to the active route, how would we support `startTransition`, which renders the _next_ page concurrently in the background, and presumably wants the _new_ `query` value? My approach in #33875 is working on addressing this from this perspective, i.e. just creating a new public instance for each route/navigation, instead of generating a new one each time `AppContainer` is rerendered. The approach in this PR is probably fine to unblock for now, but I don't think it'll work for concurrent rendering.

body:
I'm wondering if the mention of server components above could be confusing (in that this doc does not address server components). Tried to clarify a bit:

```suggestion
React 18 will include architectural improvements to React server-side rendering (SSR) performance. This means you can use `Suspense` in your React components in streaming SSR mode and React will render them on the server and send them through HTTP streams. 

It's worth noting that another experimental feature, React Server Components, is based on streaming.  You can read more about server components related streaming APIs like `next/streaming` [here](). However, this guide focuses on basic React 18 streaming.
```
author: kara

author: kara

body: It would be good to add a high-level description of what it means to turn on streaming (because the term is pretty overloaded and possibly unclear). What actually changes if you set this flag to true?  e.g. if you add Suspense without this flag, Suspense fallbacks will be flushed once and it will be full client-side-rendering. If you turn the flag on, the suspending will actually happen on the server and content / script tags will be sent through as the boundaries resolve. Also - what are the possible advantages of turning on streaming?
author: kara

```suggestion
Server Components allow us to render React components on the server. This is fundamentally different from server-side rendering (SSR) where you're pre-generating HTML on the server. With Server Components, there's **zero client-side JavaScript needed,** making page rendering faster. This improves the user experience of your application, pairing the best parts of server-rendering with client-side interactivity.
```

body:
```suggestion
Enabling streaming SSR means React renders your components into streams and the client continues receiving updates from these streams even after the initial SSR response is sent. In other words, when any suspended components resolve down the line, they are rendered on the server and streamed to the client.  With this strategy, the app can start emitting HTML even before all the data is ready, improving your app's loading performance.  As an added bonus, in streaming SSR mode, the client will also use selective hydration strategy to prioritize component hydration which based on user interaction.
```

```suggestion
Server Components allow us to render React components on the server. This is fundamentally different from server-side rendering (SSR) where you're pre-generating HTML on the server. With Server Components, there's **zero client-side JavaScript needed**, making page rendering faster. This improves the user experience of your application, pairing the best parts of server-rendering with client-side interactivity.
```

Also, should we be more clear about "zero client-side JavaScript"? Because there are necessary JS that will be shipped such as React's runtime, so technically it's not "zero" and it confuses people ([ref](https://github.com/vercel/next-rsc-demo/issues/11#issuecomment-988128075)).

I'll try to rephrase it as

```diff
- With Server Components, there's zero client-side JavaScript needed
+ Server Components won't increase your bundle size and ship zero JavaScript to the client
```

body: The need for `{suspense: true}` makes it feel second-class. Do we plan to flip the default at some point in the future?
author: gaearon

body:
The issue is that currently that would be a breaking change without an incremental upgrade path: your application using `next/dynamic` would break when upgrading to React 18 unless you add suspense boundaries first, but you can't add suspense boundaries until you upgrade to React 18. It also changes the semantics a bit, since pre-Suspense, the loading state is associated with the `next/dynamic` component, rather than the parent where the component is rendered.

Changing the default in the future is a possibility, but it's likely a major version away. Some shorter term options might be:
* If we could detect that we're rendering inside a `<Suspense>` boundary, we could assume you want the suspense behavior
* If we could somehow port the preloading behavior to `React.lazy`, we could push users toward that instead, when using React>=18

Both of these options would probably require some support in React though.
author: devknoll

body: It occurred to me that with https://github.com/facebook/react/pull/23304 and https://github.com/facebook/react/pull/23267 this might not actually be a breaking change anymore, if the transition semantics apply to `root.render()` as well... Would need to investigate further though.
author: devknoll

body:
I think what we could theoretically do is use suspense by default during SSR and initial hydration if you're using React>=18, since that behavior shouldn't change (after the next RC).

For client-side navigations that would still be at risk of suspending, we could synchronously render the legacy non-Suspense based fallback that you provide with `next/dynamic`, and then also warn that you should add a suspense boundary and move your fallback there instead.

Let me think about it some more.

author: xhoantran

body:
Both demos appear to be matching the dynamic route correctly, the problem with the `pathname` approach is that it will match multiple `ActiveLink`s for a dynamic route but different `as` values when it should only match one or the other e.g. 

`<ActiveLink href='/[slug]' as='/first'>` and `<ActiveLink href='/[slug]' as='/second'>`

It's also preferrable for a user to only provide `href` now for dynamic routes since it will be matched automatically and in that case the `pathname` would never match

It also matches the other names (`optimizeCss`, `optimizeFonts`) but let me know if you still think I should change the name to something like `workerOptimization`.
author: housseindjirdeh

body:
> Is the concern its not required in all pages?

Exactly. I was loading it in SSR *and* in CSR with the idea being that if there's no worker scripts on the initial page (`<script strategy="worker">`) but in a subsequent client-side transition; then load it only when necessary.

The snippet itself is just ~1KB though, so this was pretty unnecessary. I just removed this client-side logic completely. :)

> Maybe we can bundle it and save the extra RTT and justify loading it everytime

I did bundle it using `ncc` in the first iteration of the PR, but @adamdbradley (author of Partytown) provided some good feedback and mentioned that since it's still an experimental library—it might be better to allow users to install any version of the dependency themselves. In addition, the library's [publish size](https://packagephobia.com/result?p=%40builder.io%2Fpartytown) isn't trivial (328 KB) so it likely makes more sense for the user to install it only when needed.
author: housseindjirdeh

body: I chose to pass through objectFit and objectPosition as styles to the img. This isn't specifically called out in the documentation, but it feels like it would be a common expectation that if `objectFit="cover"` works on images in the `fill` layout, it should work in the `raw` layout. I'm open to changing this though
author: atcastle

body:
I'm including `raw` with the `w` type layouts because I think the main use case for raw images are going to be places where people are manually resizing the images using styling, and therefore we'll want to have a full `srcset`.

I considered trying to inspect the properties of `style` if it exists, to make optimizations to the `srcset`, but I think that would probably be more complexity than it's worth.

Could we also add some examples for some reasonable use cases for "raw"? And how to avoid gotchas that could cause CLS?

```suggestion
> An additional `raw` layout mode is provided which removes the wrapper element and allows the `style` prop to be used. This mode still requires `height` and `width` and is recommended only for advanced use cases that aren't covered by the primary layout modes. When this mode is selected, it's possible to style the component in a way that causes CLS, so proceed with caution.
```

author: kara

body: Maybe we can see if there's demand for that first? I think we should try to avoid adding two new layout modes if we can, to keep it simple
author: kara

body: Curious why we need to create the `isRaw` variable. Can we not check if `layout === 'raw'` here like we have elsewhere?
author: kara

body: "simple" as in engineering overhead or simple as in too-many-choices-for-the-user? Because I think the engineering overhead here would be small to add support for both. Also, I feel like if I saw `layout=raw` as a user, I would assume it mimics a raw `` as close as possible which would be closest to `layout=fixed` rather than `layout=responsive`.
author: styfle

body: Simple as in too-many-choices-for-the-user (sorry - my comment was pretty vague). I think we have to be careful about adding a layout mode for each expectation a user could have because each mode adds mental load for new users. I think it's fine if we make a choice here and document it clearly. If we find that people's expectations are different than we expected, we can always add more modes (harder to take them away)
author: kara

body:
I'm open to either way.

From the performance side of things, it would be better not to spread these styles when we don't have to, so omitting `objectFit` and `objectPosition` might make sense (we could warn).

It also might make it easier to reuse the existing code like I suggested in [this comment](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/34339/files#r807160245)

I think the missing element here is going to be providing a warning via performanceObserver if the image is rendered much smaller than intrinsic. This will allow us to alert people if they ought to be using `sizes` on their `layout="raw"` image.

body:
This is a highly wanted feature since `next/image` launch.

I think there's a misinterpretation between what `raw` means for users and what `raw` means for this PR.

What we, the users, need is a `raw` as in:

1. No **_forced_** NextJs styles
2. We need to be able to set `priority` and preload it if necessary
3. We want the blurry placeholder

We simply don't want the actual `layout` because it either bloat the DOM or messes up how we position the image in our designs.

At launch, the styles were even using `!important` to make sure we couldn't overwrite it... it was so bad some of us simply gave up on `next/image` altogether.

Personally, I completely rewrote the client-side `next/image` to be able to use it. Here's the Gist and there's a section that enumerates some improvements on the ideas of `next/image`: https://gist.github.com/TheThirdRace/7f270a786629f119b57d1b2227a4b113

I retained pretty much all your functionalities without using any `layout`. It shows that pretty much everything you've done was great, the only bad step was to tie in the `layout` as a **_forced_** option because that removed any flexibility and increased DOM bloating to the roof.
author: TheThirdRace

body:
`Insert the image element with no responsive behavior` is misleading here. Point in case, the very next column describe how it auto-adjust its source according to responsive space available.

I think it would be better to say `Insert the image element without any wrappers` or `without NextJs layouts applied` .

The "responsive" aspect you're referring to is more about NextJs layouts than what people are expecting "responsive" to mean in an image context. That's why I would leave that out of the description so nobody confuses `NextJs responsive layouts` with `responsive images`, which are 2 totally different things.
author: TheThirdRace

body: Technically, `objectFit` and `objectPosition` could easily be passed to the `style` attribute on the `` component when `layout === 'raw'`. With the current implementation, you're creating 2 methods to specify `objectFit` and `objectPosition`: 1. Pass the actual properties to the component 2. Pass some CSS in the `style` attribute This has some consequences: 1. The errors thrown creates frustration because you already passed the properties, but the component now wants you to use a second method instead 2. It creates confusion, you might be looking at properties when you should be looking at CSS... I think it would be simpler for users to only pass properties.
author: TheThirdRace

body:
Why remove the priority on `raw` layout?

There's  a difference between:
- `raw` as in "no NextJs responsive layout so you have control on the styling"
- `raw` as in "it's a barebone component, you do everything yourself"

The way `raw` was described is the former, not the later.

Thus `raw` should benefit from `priority` as you want to remove NextJs styling, not remove the optimization you'd get from preloading if you pass the prop.
author: TheThirdRace

body: Thanks for pointing this out--it was just an oversight on my part. The intention was always for `raw` to work with `priority`. I've fixed this and added a test for it.
author: atcastle

body:
An earlier version of this PR did exactly what you're suggesting (handling objectFit and objectPosition when passed as a prop), but after some discussion with @styfle I ended up changing it to the current method. I think either way it's not ideal, but this way is at least consistent in that when in raw mode all styles are applied via the `style` prop. Having some styles automatically translated from props to styles seems like it could create the expectation that other styles could be passed the same way.

Also, this implementation simplifies the code a bit.

It might just be me, but I wouldn't underestimate what `responsive` can mean in different context.

With that said, I would tend to go more with something that mention NextJs layouts. Maybe something like `Insert the image element with no automatic layout`

The reason those props were added is because you used to not be able to use inline style:


So the workaround was adding these helper props to assist:

```jsx
<Image src={src} layout="fill" objectFit="cover" />
```

I think throwing the error for the new layout=raw to make it clear that `style` should be used instead.

Alternatively we could warn during `next dev` only.

I would add it was also added because we can't target the images easily by pure CSS selectors. Which is still a much needed feature one year after the launch of `next/image` :(

I would argue it was also a bit arbitrary that only `objectFit` and `objectPosition` were targeted to be props in this context. A class on each wrapper would have made it super easy to add all the CSS styling you want without resorting to using props, inline styles or DOM bloating. Separation of concerns is a big thing for a lot of people.
author: TheThirdRace

body:
You could use the height/width for setting the aspect ratio so the browser knows what space to reserve to reduce CLS.

The browser will try to optimize before any sizer or padding trick. From my experience, the CLS is still there, but almost zero. The SVG sizer and padding tricks are really only needed to reduce your already low 0.05 CLS to 0...

I'm not 100% sure on this one, but if you keep the height/width, I think the browser will use the calculated aspect ratio and the available space to choose the right version to download from the `srcset`.

What I'm 100% sure, removing the height/width will not benefit you in any way. Furthermore, people using the `raw` layout are most likely gonna want to control their image entirely, which means removing a value they specifically passed is most likely not wanted.

body: I agree with @kara , the whole purpose of `raw` is to not add layout behaviors. The "responsive" behavior from the browser is to download the right image version according to `height`, `width`, `sizes` and `srcset` attributes. Nothing should come in the way of of this. I'd even argue if a user passes `srcset` prop when in `raw` layout, the values from the user should be passed directly to the `` without any modification. I think a default of `1x`/`2x` or `640w, 750w, ...,3840w` depending if `sizes` is set or not is fine, just don't prevent people from generating their own `srcset`. What I consider should always be `640w, ...3840w` might be different for another person and vice versa. The whole point of `raw` is to give back the power to users, defaults are welcome if not forced on you.

author: atcastle

body:
What it's doing now, which I think is probably the best compromise, is adding height and width unless you use `sizes`. Sizes should only be used for responsive images, so it doesn't really make sense to include a fixed height and width. Also, having those height and width attributes on a responsive image can mess up a user's intended responsive behavior. For instance, if the user sets a responsive width without realizing that there's a fixed height still attached to the image, it will stretch out the image.

Finally, I'm now adding a `aspect-ratio` style to all raw images. As discussed in the past this still misses a couple of supported browsers, but it should go a long way toward preventing CLS in the responsive case.

TLDR: Images using raw layout with a user-provided responsive styling could (possibly but not likely) suffer from CLS on ie11 and old versions of other browsers. The share of users on such browsers will decrease over time.

body:
> Why do you use your own comments, instead of cloning it of Tester?

Because `emotion` only accepts `&comments`, the `Compiler::comments` return the `&comments`.
`Clone` `tr.commens` here will be dropped out of the `Fn`. I need another `comments` out of the `Fn` scope to make `&comments` life time longer than `emotion`.

Oh it looks like the `destination` for [this rewrite](https://github.com/Nutlope/redirect-demo/blob/b018414bbbc9cc5cb3c28ee5b0670327759f6a2f/next.config.js#L53) needs to end with a trailingSlash to match the other deployments expectation e.g. does changing it to the below resolve this?

author: ijjk

body: I think documenting that the `destination` for a rewrite needs to honor the external destination's trailingSlash config makes sense
author: ijjk

body:
Good catch, it works when you include a trailing slash for both the source and destination like this.

```js
{
  source: "/blog/:path*/",
  destination: `${BLOG_URL}/blog/:path*/`,
},
```

 I'm assuming this is better than the `:path(.+)` wildcard so I just updated the PR to reflect that.
author: Nutlope

body:

body: This should be using `toMatch` for an exact match, not `toContain`, or a better alternative is `toMatchInlineSnapshot` so you can drop the extra variable.  https://jestjs.io/docs/snapshot-testing#inline-snapshots
author: balazsorban44

body: Let's make the assertion exact since we are trying to verify that there is no leftover in the output.
author: balazsorban44

body:
I don't think that either`toMatchInlineSnapshot` or `toMatch` would be the right choice here, since the `stderr` string contains a lot of machine specific information such as absolute paths
i.e. 
`at new Input (/Users/baruchadi/projects/next/next.js/packages/next/node_modules/postcss/lib/input.js:24:13)`

and given that the string is about 60 lines long and has more than than 6500 characters (see example value here: https://gist.github.com/baruchadi/81be9d5b4d1f45d6f1f22b2195777a9a), I believe that `.toContain` would be our best bet at the moment. 
We can do `.toMatch` with some regex, but that would require us to make a lot of assumptions and the result would be very similar to `.toContain`.

**Note** that in the code, i'm checking for two new lines before and after our clean message. this should guarantee that the stack was removed from above and below our message.

see the diff between our error message and the original unedited one 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3421731/157109430-caa4eb03-08a3-4077-84e6-7ffb877cabde.png)
author: baruchadi


author: leerob

body: What does best effort mean here? Does it look at the file extension? How would it fail?
author: leerob

body: The buffer is analyzed using [is-animated](https://www.npmjs.com/package/is-animated) package which returns true/false. If it returns true, then we bypass. If it returns false, then we optimize the image.
author: styfle

body: This is interesting, i didnt know that we dont optimize animated images. So a site with lots of GIFs wont benefit from our optimizations
author: molebox

body: In most cases, animated images are not source images. Instead, they are typically exported from some other source such as a video. Most tools that export to animated gif offer a compression option so theres very few cases where a user would even want to optimize an animated image anyway (so far no one has requested it).

This would definitely be useful to have! Unfortunately, the ESLint Node API doesn't support `--output-file` as an option (see the list of options [here](https://eslint.org/docs/developer-guide/nodejs-api#-new-eslintoptions)).

If you would still like to see this flag supported, you'll need to include it as a [top-level option](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/cli/next-lint.ts#L19) and then provide some custom logic for it within the `runLintCheck` method. You can see a similar example of how I added support for the `--quiet` flag in this [commit](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/26280/commits/8866553ad55af772ba8d49504652381c4b78ceff).

If this is too much work to do, you can always fallback to using the default ESLint CLI to achieve your outcome (`npx eslint pages -format json --output-file test.json`).

Let me know if you have any thoughts or questions!

body:
By "image pipeline" here I was thinking of whatever various processes happen to the image between them being introduced to the system and them being served to the user. "Introduced to the system" could be an editor uploading an image to a CMS, or a user uploading UGC images, or even just a dev adding an image to the filesystem.

I think the takeaway from this bullet point was supposed to just be "consider if there are other places in your architecture where you could remove size-uncertainty from your images"

This would give users the opportunity to try out the bleeding-edge at the expense of poor Safari support (which is [15% global usage](https://caniuse.com/loading-lazy-attr) today). Once adoption increases, we can look at making this the default 👍

body:
```suggestion
> **Why does Next.js use 307 and 308?** Traditionally a 302 was used for a temporary redirect, and a 301 for a permanent redirect, but many browsers changed the request method of the redirect to `GET`, regardless of the original method. For example, if the browser made a request to `POST /v1/users` which returned status code `302` with location `/v2/users`, the subsequent request might be `GET /v2/users` instead of the expected `POST /v2/users`. Next.js uses the 307 temporary redirect, and 308 permanent redirect status codes to explicitly preserve the request method used.
```

body:
```suggestion
If the page is using `getServerSideProps` or `getInitialProps`, then it will use the default `Cache-Control` header configured by `next start` in order to prevent accidental caching of responses that cannot be cached. If you want a different cache behavior while using SSR you can use `res.setHeader('Cache-Control', 'value_you_prefer')`.

If the page is using `getStaticProps` or automatic static optimization, then it will have s-maxage=REVALIDATE_SECONDS, stale-while-revalidate or if revalidate is not used s-maxage=31536000, stale-while-revalidate.
```
author: balazsorban44

body: Thanks!
author: balazsorban44

body: The `stale-while-revalidate` header is not set for pages that are automatically statically optimized, only pages that use `getStaticProps` and return a `revalidate` value.

body:
no, it's set by cargo (https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/environment-variables.html), as well as other env var in this 
build script (`TARGET`).

> OUT_DIR — If the package has a build script, this is set to the folder where the build script should place its output

> TARGET — the target triple that is being compiled for. Native code should be compiled for this triple.

These env var should be set as long as build goes through cargo (napi-rs cli wraps cargo as well). If someone uses rustc directly to build package it'll cause compilation error, which is highly unlikely in next-swc's case.

body:
My understanding is that we no longer support the same method for previewing of a draft post, so there's no longer a getPreviewPostBySlug method.

As for the on-demand ISR - that sounds like a very cool feature! Unfortunately, the main next.js developer that we had for creating the starter is not available, and I'm really hesitant to change anything that's going to affect functionality on that level, as I'm not a next.js developer (or even js developer :sweat_smile: myself). 

We have another version of this starter in our docs and our page that we'd also have to update, as we're trying to keep the two versions (i.e., the one in our docs and the one in the next.js examples) from diverging too greatly. Our main goal with the starter was for someone to be able to set it up super-rapidly using the exact same steps across all stacks that we support, so we'd like to prevent having to set up extra environment variables, etc.

If the on-demand static ISR is an absolute requirement for updating our current example, I can forward that information to my team, but I think our preference at this time would be to not implement it if that's a possibility? It also makes the process of testing/hosting a bit more difficult, due to the need to create a production build and start the production server, and we're trying to keep these starters as easy to drop in as possible.

body:
> Thanks, I added some small comments.
> 
> Another question, what's the correct way of referring to the library? I see the directory was named `with-particles`, but the package is called `react-tsparticles`, the website https://particles.js.org/ says "TS Particles" (while the page title is tsParticles).
> 
> The reason I'm asking is that there is a https://vincentgarreau.com/particles.js/ so to not confuse developers, maybe we should call the directory `with-tsparticles`? What do you think?

`react-particles-js` was using `tsParticles` in its core since `3.0.0` but it was deprecated some weeks ago in favor of `react-tsparticles`. Using `react-particles-js` is not recommended, since it's not working anymore. I think the confusion could be avoided keeping `with-particles`.

These [CRA templates](https://github.com/matteobruni/tsparticles/tree/main/templates) are using `particles` as well (like `npx create-react-app my-app --template particles`)

body: The index is reset to zero on reload, resulting in inconsistencies in the session storage information and restoring the scroll position to an unintended position when browesr back or forward.
author: AkifumiSato

body:
1. Load `/person/1` (SSR)
```
+-----------+
| /person/1 |
| idx: 0    |
+-----------+
```
2. Move to `/person/2` (pushState)
```
+-----------+-----------+
| /person/1 | /person/2 |
| idx: 0    | idx: 1    |
+-----------+-----------+
```
3. Reload `/person/2` (SSR)
```
+-----------+-----------+
| /person/1 | /person/2 |
| idx: 0    | idx: 0    |
+-----------+-----------+
```
The `idx` value and scroll position are accidentally shared between pages.

body:
Looking at other eslint configs it seems like we might be handling this wrong and we should be using `peerDependencies` for the eslint plugins to prevent this although not sure we can change that without a major bump cc @housseindjirdeh for thoughts

`eslint-config-airbnb` https://unpkg.com/eslint-config-airbnb@19.0.4/package.json
`eslint-config-standard` https://unpkg.com/eslint-config-standard@16.0.3/package.json
author: ijjk

body:
I think our usage of [@rushstack/eslint-patch](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@rushstack/eslint-patch) prevents us from using a typical `peerDependencies` setup in favor of simplifying the workflow for developers (they only need to install a single package (`eslint-config-next`) instead of all the plugins separately).

I'd be happy to consider changing this if there are issues with this approach, but I agree it would have to be in a major version change.
author: housseindjirdeh

body:
Yeah. Our current approach seems to cause issues (see https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/35806 also) and I can see it being the ground for more in the future.

https://www.npmjs.com/package/eslint-config-airbnb recommends `npx install-peerdeps --dev eslint-config-airbnb` which will auto-install all the necessary `peerDependencies`. Could that be an option that we could bake in?
author: balazsorban44

body:
> Yeah. Our current approach seems to cause issues (see https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/35806 also) and I can see it being the ground for more the future.

In that case, then let's aim to switch it to a normal `peerDep` setup. :)

> Could that be an option that we could bake in?

Yep that looks like the best possible path forward. If possible, I would like to bake this option in first and then show a warning for folks who don't have `peerDeps` installed. That will give them enough time to switch before we introduce the breaking change of removing [@rushstack/eslint-patch](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@rushstack/eslint-patch) completely.
author: housseindjirdeh

body: I think it's actually the opposite: Next.js should be configured with a timeout that is _larger_ than the LB's timeout. See here: https://adamcrowder.net/posts/node-express-api-and-aws-alb-502/ We had 502s and set our timeout slightly higher than the LBs and that resolved them for us.
author: mAAdhaTTah

body:
See also here: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/application-load-balancers.html#connection-idle-timeout

> We also recommend that you configure the idle timeout of your application to be larger than the idle timeout configured for the load balancer. Otherwise, if the application closes the TCP connection to the load balancer ungracefully, the load balancer might send a request to the application before it receives the packet indicating that the connection is closed. If this is the case, the server refuses the request from the load balancer and then the load balancer sends an HTTP 502 Bad Gateway error to the application.

body:
I removed the input-type validation (`args` module seems to already take care of this) and replaced it with some input-range validation. Then input-range validation clamps the `--keepAliveTimeout` value to be between 0 and Infinity-1, inclusive. 

There doesn't seem to be a way to write a test for this since 1) the invalid-range validation will not throw errors and 2) the underlying `http` server is not exposed from `startServer`. If the lack of tests for this validation will hold up the PR, I can revert the commit that introduced the range-validation. Let me know.

It would be nice to get this PR in in the next canary release. Let me know if you guys need something more, @styfle  @timneutkens

```suggestion
### Keep Alive Timeout
```

author: styfle

body:
```suggestion
When deploying Next.js behind a downstream proxy (e.g. a load-balancer like AWS ELB/ALB) it's important to configure Next's underlying HTTP server with [keep-alive timeouts](https://nodejs.org/api/http.html#http_server_keepalivetimeout) that are _larger_ than the downstream proxy's timeouts. Otherwise, once a keep-alive timeout is reached for a given TCP connection, Node.js will immediately terminate that connection without notifying the downstream proxy. This results in a proxy error whenever it attempts to reuse a connection that Node.js has already terminated.
```

body:
```suggestion
React 18 includes architectural improvements to React server-side rendering (SSR) performance. This means you can use `Suspense` in your React components in streaming SSR mode and React will render content on the server and send updates through HTTP streams.
```
author: leerob

body:
```suggestion
React Server Components, an experimental feature, is based on streaming. You can read more about Server Components related streaming APIs in [`next/streaming`](/docs/api-reference/next/streaming.md). However, this guide focuses on streaming with React 18.
```
author: leerob

body:
```suggestion
## Using Streaming Server-Rendering
```
author: leerob

body:
```suggestion
If you have [React 18](/docs/advanced-features/react-18/overview) installed, there is a new experimental feature that lets you switch the page runtime between Node.js and the [Edge Runtime](/docs/api-reference/edge-runtime). Changing the runtime affects [SSR streaming](/docs/advanced-features/react-18/streaming) and [Server Components](/docs/advanced-features/react-18/server-components) features, as well.
```
author: leerob

body:
```suggestion
This option determines which runtime should be used as the default rendering runtime for all pages.
```
author: leerob

body:
```suggestion
On each page, you can optionally export a `runtime` config set to either `'nodejs'` or `'edge'`:
```
author: leerob

body:
```suggestion
When both the per-page runtime and global runtime are set, the per-page runtime overrides the global runtime. If the per-page runtime is _not_ set, the global runtime option will be used.
```
author: leerob

body:
```suggestion
All SSR pages have the ability to render components into streams and the client continues receiving updates from these streams even after the initial SSR response is sent. When any suspended components resolve down the line, they are rendered on the server and streamed to the client. This means applications can start emitting HTML even _before_ all the data is ready, improving your app's loading performance.

As an added bonus, in streaming SSR mode the client will also use selective hydration to prioritize component hydration based on user interactions, further improving performance.
```
author: leerob

body:
```suggestion
When you use Suspense in a server-rendered page, there is no extra configuration required to use streaming SSR.
```
author: leerob

body: We should probably clarify this - you could stream using the Node.js runtime if you were self-hosting.
author: leerob

body: No they don't need to "enable" streaming anymore, the rendering architecture is always enabled now. If they use the Edge runtime, they get streaming from the Edge. If they use the Node.js runtime, they will have streaming if Next.js is running in a Node.js server. And if it's running in a lambda function that doesn't support streaming, the response will be buffered and sent all at once when everything is ready.
author: shuding

body:
```suggestion
When you use Suspense in a server-rendered page, there is no extra configuration required to use streaming SSR. When deployed, streaming can be utilized through infrastructure like [Edge Functions](https://vercel.com/edge) on Vercel (with the Edge Runtime) or with a Node.js server (with the Node.js runtime). AWS Lambda Functions do not currently support streaming responses.
```

body:
So for non-redirect paths it should have the `.md` extension and points to a file in the docs folder, this is what the lint step is mainly checking for to ensure we don't forget to add manifest entries or have typos. 

For redirect paths it doesn't need to have `.md` as it's matching the path directly without checking the filesystem and the lint step doesn't check these.  The destination for the redirect can be any url so if we need to move docs to another site we can e.g. `https://vercel.com/docs/some/path` but in this case it can just be `/docs/advanced-features/middleware` since it's the same site. 

Seems like it would be good to add this to our contributing, I'll create a PR with notes on this and request your review.

```suggestion
The cache status of a page leveraging ISR can be determined by reading the value of the `x-nextjs-cache` response header. The possible values are the following:

- `MISS` - the is not in the cache (occurs at most once, on the first visit)
- `STALE` - the path is in the cache but exceeded the revalidate time so it will be updated in the background
-  `HIT` - the path is in the cache and has not exceeded the revalidate time
```

body:
Interesting, they are sensitive in `vercel.json`. I tend to think `sensitive: true` is the correct default for two reasons:

1. Avoid duplicate content (insensitive would allow 2 urls to serve the same content)
2. Avoid cache invalidation (insensitive would allow multiple cache keys for the same content)

body:
This is incorrect as per https://github.com/prettier/eslint-config-prettier#cli-helper-tool

> While the "prettier" config can disable problematic rules in "some-other-config-you-use", it cannot touch "rules"! (That’s how ESLint works – it lets you override configs you extend.) The CLI helper tool reports that "indent" conflicts with Prettier, so you can remove it. (Which is nice – simplifying your config!)

TLDR: `"indent": "error"` is an example of a rule that should be removed according to the readme.

body: Removing the message is not the way to go. It should either detect if strict mode wasn't set previously and not show that part of the message or the message can be expanded to include "if it was not set previously".
author: timneutkens

body:
I'm assuming you've misinterpreted the comment above given that the message is still removed. There are 2 options in my comment above:

- Detect if strict mode wasn't set previously and don't show the strict mode message
- Change the message to `Strict-mode is set to ${chalk.bold('false')} by default if it was not set previously`

Personally prefer the first option given that it would have the result you want only when strict mode was set already 👍

```suggestion
To ensure consistency across all pods, you can disable in-memory caching. This will inform the Next.js server to only leverage assets generated by ISR in the file system.
```

author: ijjk

body:
```suggestion
You can use this approach when deploying to container orchestrators such as [Kubernetes](https://kubernetes.io/) or [HashiCorp Nomad](https://www.nomadproject.io/). By default, generated assets will be stored in-memory on each pod. This means that each pod will have its own copy of the static files. Stale data may be shown until that specific pod is hit by a request.
```
